basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Ikhlāṣ — “Sincerity”, also called al-Tawḥīd, “The Unity” — is among the briefest and most essential sūras of the Quran. Its title — meaning sincerity, devotion, and purity together — describes its content: it is the full statement of tawḥīd, the affirmation that God is One. A widespread ḥadīth says that it is “equivalent to one-third of the Quran”: not for its length, but because the message of Divine Oneness is one-third of the Book's whole message. With Sūra 109, al-Kāfirūn — the complete denial of every false worship — the two together form the Muqashqishatān, “the two cures”. Al-Rāzī sums up its centrality: “The whole Quran is an oyster, and the pearl is this saying: Say, He, God, is One.”

versets.commentaire: qul huwa Llāhu aḥad (قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ): four words that gather the whole of tawḥīd. Huwa (هُوَ), “He”, is first a pronoun — God named without a name, as the Essence beneath every attribution; some Sufis took it as the supreme Name, and the call yā Huwa (يَا هُوَ) became an invocation. Then comes Allāh (اللَّه), the Name that gathers. Then aḥad (أَحَد), “One” — not the wāḥid (وَاحِد) of counting, which is one in a series, but the absolute Oneness that admits neither second nor division.

mots.def: to say, to utter; in the imperative: “say”, “proclaim”.
3rd person singular masculine pronoun: He; here used absolutely to designate God in His Essence, beneath every Name and attribute.
Allāh: the proper Name of God in Arabic; called by the commentators the “Gathering Name” (al-ism al-jāmiʿ), in which all the other Divine Names are contained.
aḥad: the absolute One, the indivisible Oneness which admits neither second nor partition; distinct from wāḥid (the one of counting).

versets.commentaire: al-Ṣamad (الصَّمَد) is a unique Name: it appears only here in the Quran. Its lexical sense first evokes what is full, dense, without hollow — the imperishable. Theologically it means the One toward whom every being turns in its need, the One on whom all things depend while He depends on none. Al-Zamakhsharī puts it thus: al-Ṣamad is the One who is sought (man ṣumida ilayhi, مَنْ صُمِدَ إِلَيْه) when one is in need — the Master to whom every being turns, and who needs none.

mots.def: Allāh: the proper Name of God in Arabic.
al-Ṣamad: a Divine Name unique to this sūra; lexically, what is full, imperishable, without hollow; theologically, the One toward whom every being turns in its need, on whom all things depend.

versets.trad: He begets not, nor is He begotten,
versets.commentaire: The verse states pure transcendence: no divine lineage, no son or daughter, no ancestor. The Quran repeats it: “How should He have a child when He has no consort, when He has created all things?” (6:101). The sūra first addresses the pre-Islamic Arabs who ascribed daughters to God, but its true reach is metaphysical: God does not beget, because that which is begotten is born and dies; and He is not begotten, because He is without origin.

mots.def: negative particle: not; with the apocopated imperfect it negates a past fact.
walada: to beget, to bring forth, to give birth; with lam, absolute negation.
mots.gram: imperfect apocopated verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: passive of walada: to be begotten, to be born; with lam: He has not been begotten.
mots.gram: imperfect apocopated passive, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: and none is like unto Him.”
versets.commentaire: No created thing resembles Him. “Nothing is like unto Him,” says the Quran elsewhere (42:11). The attributes given to creatures — living, seeing, hearing — God also has, but in an infinitely other sense. And this transcendence is not for that a distance: utterly other, He is also the most near — “wheresoever you turn, there is the Face of God” (2:115). The sūra closes upon the word with which it began — aḥad (أَحَد), “One” — like a ring drawn shut.

mots.def: kāna: to be; here in the apocopated imperfect with lam: he is not, there is not.
mots.gram: imperfect apocopated verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition li- + suffix pronoun 3rd pers. masc. sing.: for Him, to Him.
kufuw: equal, equivalent, comparable; what would stand opposite as a matching measure.
aḥad: one, anyone; in negative context: not one, none. The word closes the sūra it opened in v. 1.
